topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the resume spread across my tiny apartment table, the font mocking me with its professional sheen. Sarah had told me, with such earnest enthusiasm over late-night pizza, that she was a senior architect at a prestigious firm downtown. Now, the details felt like shards of glass under my fingernails. Every casual mention of blueprints or client meetings sounded hollow, a carefully constructed facade crumbling around me. My hands were trembling so badly I had to grip the edge of the table to keep from knocking over the stack of papers. I needed to know the truth; the silence in my head was screaming for an answer. I picked up my phone, the screen illuminating a landscape of unanswered texts, and felt a cold, heavy knot tighten in my chest.

[story 2]

"So, where are you, really?" The question slipped out, rough and unexpected, tasting metallic on my tongue. Liam’s eyes, usually so warm, flickered away, a quick, evasive movement that sent a spike of panic through me. I watched the way his jaw clenched, the sudden stiffness in his shoulders. It wasn't the usual easy banter; this was guarded, defensive. I leaned forward, my voice dropping to a low, intense murmur. "Don't play games with me, Liam. I saw the LinkedIn profile. It doesn't match up." The air thickened instantly, heavy with unspoken accusations. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of sheer frustration that made my throat feel tight. I needed him to look at me, to admit what was happening, because the uncertainty was eroding my composure piece by piece.

[story 3]

I kept replaying the conversation in my head, dissecting every word Sarah had used to paint her professional picture. It was all so polished, so assured, and yet, something fundamental felt off. A tremor ran through my hands as I scrolled through archived emails I’d somehow managed to access. The official correspondence was sparse, sterile, lacking the vibrant energy she claimed to possess. A profound sense of being utterly alone washed over me. I paced the cramped living room, the floorboards groaning under my hurried steps. Each footfall seemed to echo the frantic rhythm of my own anxiety. I stopped by the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to force a clarity that refused to materialize.
